preamble: The shift from [tʰ] to [t] often occurs as a result of Grassmann's Law, a process of dissimilatory deaspiration in Ancient Greek and Sanskrit. If an aspirated consonant is followed by another aspirated consonant in the next syllable, the first one loses its aspiration.

examples.note: Grassmann's Law: The first [tʰ] in the reduplicated present of 'tʰē-' (to put) deaspirates to [t] because it is followed by another [tʰ].

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
